tasks.prompt:
Warmup — Inspect a repository and create your first feature branch.

In any local Git repository (or run `git init my-demo && cd my-demo && echo 'hello' > README.md && git add . && git commit -m 'init'` to create one), do the following:
1. Check which branch you are on.
2. Create a new branch called `add-greeting` and switch to it in a single command.
3. Confirm the new branch now exists and is currently active.
tasks.starter:
# Run these setup commands first if you don't have a repo ready:
git init my-demo
cd my-demo
echo 'hello' > README.md
git add .
git commit -m 'init'

# Now complete the three steps described in the prompt.
tasks.solution:
# Step 1 – see the current branch
git branch
# Output: * main  (the asterisk marks the active branch)

# Step 2 – create and switch in one command
git checkout -b add-greeting
# Equivalent modern form: git switch -c add-greeting

# Step 3 – verify
git branch
# Output:
#   * add-greeting
#     main
tasks.explanation: `git branch` lists all local branches and highlights the active one with an asterisk. `git checkout -b <name>` is a shorthand for two commands at once: `git branch <name>` (create) followed by `git checkout <name>` (switch). After the command, `git branch` should show both `main` and `add-greeting`, with the asterisk on `add-greeting`, confirming you are now on the new isolated line of work.
hints: Run `git branch` first — the branch with `*` in front is where you currently are.
The `-b` flag in `git checkout -b <name>` means 'create AND switch'; omitting `-b` would only switch to an existing branch.
If you see 'fatal: not a git repository', you haven't initialised Git yet — run the setup commands in the starter block first.

tasks.prompt:
Core — Commit changes on the feature branch and observe branch isolation.

Continuing on the `add-greeting` branch you created in Task 1:
1. Create a file called `greeting.txt` containing the text `Hello, DevOps!`.
2. Stage it and commit it with the message `Add greeting file`.
3. Switch back to `main` and list the files in the directory. Confirm that `greeting.txt` is absent on `main`.
4. Switch back to `add-greeting` and confirm the file is present again.
tasks.starter:
# You should already be on the add-greeting branch.
# Verify with:
git branch

# Then complete the four steps.
tasks.solution:
# Step 1 – create the file
echo 'Hello, DevOps!' > greeting.txt

# Step 2 – stage and commit
git add greeting.txt
git commit -m 'Add greeting file'

# Step 3 – switch to main and check files
git checkout main
ls          # on Windows PowerShell: dir
# greeting.txt should NOT appear here

# Step 4 – switch back and confirm
git checkout add-greeting
ls          # on Windows PowerShell: dir
# greeting.txt IS present again
tasks.explanation: `git add` moves the file into the staging area — a holding zone where you build up the exact set of changes you want to snapshot. `git commit` then freezes that staging area into a permanent, uniquely identified snapshot. The demonstration of switching to `main` and back is the core lesson: branches store their own history in parallel. Until you merge `add-greeting` into `main`, `main` has no knowledge of `greeting.txt`. This isolation is what lets many developers work simultaneously without colliding.
hints: If `git status` shows 'nothing to commit', double-check that `greeting.txt` was actually created — try `cat greeting.txt` (or `type greeting.txt` on Windows).
`git add .` stages *all* changed files in the current directory; `git add greeting.txt` stages only that specific file. Both work here.
After switching to `main`, if you still see `greeting.txt`, check `git log --oneline` — the commit may have landed on `main` because you forgot to switch to `add-greeting` before committing.

tasks.prompt:
Core — Simulate a pull-request review cycle, then merge into main.

This task simulates the full PR flow locally (no remote required). Continue in the same repository:
1. On `add-greeting`, add a second commit: append the line `Welcome to the team.` to `greeting.txt` and commit with the message `Expand greeting`.
2. Switch to `main` and merge `add-greeting` into it using `git merge`.
3. Verify the merge: run `git log --oneline` and confirm both commits from `add-greeting` are now in `main`'s history, plus any previous commits.
4. View the final content of `greeting.txt` on `main`.
tasks.starter:
# Make sure you are on add-greeting before Step 1.
git checkout add-greeting
git branch   # should show * add-greeting
tasks.solution:
# Step 1 – second commit on the feature branch
echo 'Welcome to the team.' >> greeting.txt
git add greeting.txt
git commit -m 'Expand greeting'

# Step 2 – switch to main and merge
git checkout main
git merge add-greeting
# Git will perform a fast-forward merge because main has no new commits.
# Output example:
#   Updating abc1234..def5678
#   Fast-forward
#    greeting.txt | 2 ++
#    1 file changed, 2 insertions(+)

# Step 3 – inspect history
git log --oneline
# Expected output (newest first):
#   def5678 Expand greeting
#   abc1234 Add greeting file
#   <hash>  init

# Step 4 – read the file
cat greeting.txt
# Output:
#   Hello, DevOps!
#   Welcome to the team.
tasks.explanation: When `main` has not moved since the branch was created, Git performs a **fast-forward** merge: it simply advances `main`'s pointer to the tip of the feature branch without creating an extra merge commit. The result is a perfectly linear history. In a real GitHub workflow you would push the branch, open a PR, get a code review, and then click 'Merge pull request' — which does this same `git merge` operation on the server side. The review step (not reproducible locally without a collaborator) is what makes the flow safe: nothing reaches `main` without being seen.
hints: Use `>>` (double redirect) to *append* to `greeting.txt`, not `>` (single redirect) which would overwrite it.
If `git merge` opens a text editor for a commit message, type `:q` (Vim) or close the editor window — Git will complete the merge with the default message.
`git log --oneline --all` shows commits from all branches if you want to compare `main` and `add-greeting` histories side by side.

tasks.prompt:
Application — Introduce a deliberate merge conflict and resolve it.

This task replicates the most common real-world Git challenge. Start fresh in the same repository (you can reuse it; `main` should contain `greeting.txt`):
1. From `main`, create a new branch `fix-greeting`. On it, change the first line of `greeting.txt` to `Hi, DevOps World!` and commit with message `Update greeting on fix branch`.
2. Switch back to `main` (without merging). Change the first line of `greeting.txt` to `Hello, everyone!` and commit with message `Update greeting on main`.
3. Now, still on `main`, run `git merge fix-greeting`. Git will report a conflict.
4. Open `greeting.txt`, read the conflict markers, decide on the final text for line 1 (write `Hello, DevOps World!` as the resolved line), remove all conflict markers, then save.
5. Stage the resolved file and create the resolution commit.
6. Confirm the conflict is gone: run `git log --oneline` and `cat greeting.txt`.
tasks.starter:
# Confirm you are on main and greeting.txt exists
git checkout main
cat greeting.txt
# Proceed with the steps above.
tasks.solution:
# Step 1 – branch fix-greeting and edit line 1
git checkout -b fix-greeting
# Overwrite only line 1 (the simplest way for this exercise):
printf 'Hi, DevOps World!\nWelcome to the team.\n' > greeting.txt
git add greeting.txt
git commit -m 'Update greeting on fix branch'

# Step 2 – switch to main and make a conflicting edit
git checkout main
printf 'Hello, everyone!\nWelcome to the team.\n' > greeting.txt
git add greeting.txt
git commit -m 'Update greeting on main'

# Step 3 – merge and observe the conflict
git merge fix-greeting
# Git output:
#   Auto-merging greeting.txt
#   CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in greeting.txt
#   Automatic merge failed; fix conflicts and then commit the result.

# Step 4 – inspect and resolve
cat greeting.txt
# Contents will look like:
#   <<<<<<< HEAD
#   Hello, everyone!
#   =======
#   Hi, DevOps World!
#   >>>>>>> fix-greeting
#   Welcome to the team.
#
# Edit the file so it contains exactly:
#   Hello, DevOps World!
#   Welcome to the team.
#
# Using a one-liner to write the resolved version:
printf 'Hello, DevOps World!\nWelcome to the team.\n' > greeting.txt

# Step 5 – stage and commit the resolution
git add greeting.txt
git commit -m 'Resolve merge conflict in greeting.txt'

# Step 6 – verify
git log --oneline
# Shows the resolution commit, both conflicting commits, and earlier history
cat greeting.txt
# Hello, DevOps World!
# Welcome to the team.
tasks.explanation: A merge conflict occurs when two branches have edited the **same line(s)** of the same file. Git cannot guess which version is correct, so it pauses the merge and inserts markers into the file: `<<<<<<< HEAD` marks the start of *your* branch's version, `=======` is the divider, and `>>>>>>> <branch>` marks the end of the incoming version. Your job as the developer is to read both sides, decide on the correct final content, delete the markers entirely, then tell Git the conflict is resolved with `git add` + `git commit`. Leaving any marker (`<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>`) in the file is a common mistake — it means the markers end up in production code. The resolution commit recorded in `git log` provides a permanent, auditable record of exactly what decision was made and why (reflected in the commit message).
hints: Run `git status` after the failed merge — it lists 'both modified' files so you know exactly which ones need fixing.
VS Code highlights conflict blocks with colour and shows 'Accept Current Change / Accept Incoming Change / Accept Both' buttons, which is often easier than editing the markers by hand.
If you want to abort the merge entirely and start over, run `git merge --abort` — this restores everything to the state before you ran `git merge`.
After editing the file, double-check with `cat greeting.txt` that no `<`, `=`, or `>` marker lines remain before you run `git add`.
